title: SEGMENT 13 — Why fish: vertebrate development you can watch

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Why fish: vertebrate development you can watch
Let me spend a segment on the specific biological advantages of zebrafish for developmental biology, because they're worth dwelling on.
Once you see what you can do with a fish embryo, the appeal becomes obvious.
A zebrafish embryo, fertilized at time zero, looks like this.
Hour 1: a single cell sitting on top of a yolk.
Hour 4: a small ball of about a thousand cells.
Hour 6: a flat disk of cells starting to spread over the yolk.
Hour 10: gastrulation, cells moving inward and outward to form germ layers.
Hour 14: the body axis has formed, the head and tail are visible.
Hour 24: a recognizable little fish, with a beating heart, twitching muscles, and visible eyes.
Hour 72: the fish hatches out of the chorion and starts swimming.
At every one of these stages, you can see what's happening, in real time, in a live embryo, under a microscope, without dissection.
That is a vertebrate biologist's dream.
Mouse embryos at the equivalent stages are buried inside the mother's uterus, accessible only by dissection that kills the embryo.
Frog embryos are larger and easier to access but slower-developing.
Chicken embryos are accessible through an egg window but you can only do this once per embryo.
Zebrafish, by contrast, give you continuous, real-time, non-destructive imaging of every stage of vertebrate development.
You can take a movie of a beating heart at hour 30.
You can track individual neurons as they migrate through the developing brain.
You can watch blood cells emerge from the dorsal aorta at the moment of definitive hematopoiesis.
A few specific advantages worth naming:
Live imaging of cardiac development.
Zebrafish embryos can survive for the first few days of development without a functional circulation, because they're small enough that oxygen reaches their tissues by diffusion.
So mutants with major cardiac defects, which would be embryonic-lethal in mice at very early stages, survive long enough in zebrafish for you to study the underlying gene functions.
The molecular genetics of heart development — gata4, nkx2.5, the heart-tube formation machinery — has been substantially worked out in zebrafish for exactly this reason.
Live imaging of neuronal migration.
You can put a fluorescent protein under the control of a neuron-specific promoter and watch individual neurons crawl through the developing brain over hours of microscopy.
The molecular logic of axon guidance, dendrite branching, and circuit formation has been imaged in zebrafish in ways simply not possible in mouse.
Hematopoiesis.
The developmental origins of blood cells, including the emergence of hematopoietic stem cells from the dorsal aorta, were imaged for the first time in zebrafish.
The molecular pathways involved turned out to be conserved with mammalian hematopoiesis, and zebrafish are now a major model for blood disorders.
Regeneration.
Adult zebrafish can regenerate fins, heart, retina, and parts of the spinal cord after injury.
Mammals can't.
Why?

Working that out in fish — and figuring out which signaling pathways are activated during fin regeneration but suppressed in mammalian wound healing — is one of the great open questions and a major research frontier.
The technical infrastructure for fish genetics has grown over the past four decades.
Methods for microinjection — injecting DNA, RNA, or other molecules into single-cell embryos — became routine in the 1990s.
Transgenic fish, with fluorescent reporters under tissue-specific promoters, are now standard.
Imaging facilities use multiphoton and light-sheet microscopy to capture entire embryos in three dimensions over time.
The "Zebrafish Information Network," ZFIN, catalogues every published mutant, every reagent, every protocol.
The big idea: the zebrafish is the only animal in which you can watch a vertebrate develop, cell by cell, from fertilization to hatching, under a microscope, without dissecting anything.
That property — visible development of a conserved vertebrate body plan — is unique.
And it's the reason the fish became the developmental biology workhorse for vertebrate-specific questions that worms and flies couldn't address.
